title: Cat Ingrown Claw in Paw Pad: Triage and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn how to identify and manage an ingrown cat claw. Understand the urgency level and why professional veterinary care is recommended.
content_html:
TL;DR: An ingrown claw occurs when a cat's nail curls into the paw pad, requiring veterinary care within 24–48 hours to treat pain and prevent infection. While usually not an emergency, seek immediate care if you notice severe swelling, pus, or a loss of appetite.

What is an ingrown cat claw and how does it affect my pet's health?

An ingrown claw occurs when a cat's nail grows too long and curls back until it punctures the paw pad. This condition is most common in senior cats who may have thicker nails or are less active with scratching, as well as in cats with polydactyl paws. The nail acts as a foreign body, causing pain, inflammation, and potentially leading to a bacterial infection.

How urgent is it if my cat has an ingrown claw?

  • An ingrown claw is considered a low-urgency medical issue that is uncomfortable and painful but not typically a life-threatening emergency.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours for professional removal and treatment.
  • The urgency increases if you notice extreme swelling of the leg, significant pus, or if your cat stops eating, which may indicate a systemic infection.

How does sending a photo help my veterinarian triage an ingrown claw?

  • A clear, high-resolution photo allows the veterinary team to see the specific angle of the claw and the condition of the surrounding skin.
  • Photos help the team determine if the claw has fully penetrated the pad and identify visible signs of infection, such as discharge or significant redness.
  • This visual information allows the clinic to prioritize your cat's visit appropriately based on the severity of the condition.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cases of ingrown claw in the paw pad, treatment must be aimed at removing pressure from the affected area. Skillful therapeutic claw trimming is highly effective to lower the bearing surface. A lift can be applied to the unaffected claw, removing weight-bearing from the affected region. If the wound is obviously contaminated, the lesion should be packed with a hygroscopic mixture (50% magnesium sulfate and 50% glycerin) and bandaged. If necrosis is detected, a portion of the toe apex may require removal under regional anesthesia. Systemic antibiotics are advised.

Chapter: Surgery, Orthopedics, Integument

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1079)

faqs.question: Can I pull the claw out myself?
faqs.answer: It is not recommended to remove an embedded claw at home. It is very painful, and if the nail is deeply lodged, removing it could cause significant bleeding or risk leaving a piece of the nail behind.

faqs.question: How can I prevent this from happening again?
faqs.answer: Regular nail trims every 2 to 4 weeks are the best prevention. For senior cats, check their paws weekly as their claws often become thicker and less brittle with age.

faqs.question: What are the signs of an infected paw pad?
faqs.answer: Common signs of infection include a foul odor, yellow or green discharge (pus), heat coming from the paw, and the cat constantly licking or biting at the area.
